result:
Done. All three operations are implemented in `CsvRow.calr` and the project builds cleanly (2 of the 10 build cycles used).

What I added to the `CsvRow` module, alongside the untouched `NeedsQuoting`:

- **`EscapeField(str) -> str`** (`§F{f002}`, pure) — calls `NeedsQuoting`; when quoting is needed it doubles internal quotes with `(replace field "\"" "\"\"")` and wraps the result in double-quotes, otherwise returns the field verbatim.
- **`JoinRow(List<str>) -> str`** (`§F{f003}`, pure) — escapes each field via `EscapeField` and joins with commas using a first-element flag; the empty list yields the empty string.
- **`SplitRow(str) -> List<str>`** (`§F{f004}`, `§E{mut}` since it builds a list with `§PUSH`) — a single-pass scanner: a field starting with `"` is consumed to the next un-doubled quote (doubled quotes contribute one literal `"`, commas/CR/LF inside are content), unquoted fields end at the next comma or end of line, and a final push makes the empty line parse to one empty field and a trailing comma yield a trailing empty field.

One wrinkle worth noting: the signature type `[str]` compiles to `string[]`, but the spec pins `List<string>`, so I used `List<str>` in the Calor signatures — the generated C# is exactly `string JoinRow(List<string>)` and `List<string> SplitRow(string)`.

Beyond the clean build, I verified behavior at runtime with a throwaway console app in /tmp (deleted afterward, nothing added to the project): all escaping cases, `JoinRow([]) == ""`, the empty-line and trailing-comma parses, and `SplitRow(JoinRow(fields)) == fields` round-trips over twelve field lists covering quotes, commas, CR/LF, empties, and quote-at-boundary cases — all passed.
